title: Fitting and selecting models
text: epifitter provides functions fit (non-flexible) two-parameter population dynamics models disease progress curve (DPC) data: exponential, monomolecular, logistic Gompertz. goal fitting models DPCs estimate numerically meaningful parameters: y0 represents primary inoculum r, representing apparent infection rate. Hence, importance choosing model best describe epidemics better understand proterties compare epidemics. Two approaches can used obtain parameters: Linear regression models fitted specific transformation disease data according models. Non-linear regression models fitted original data approaches available epifitter. simplest way fit models single epidemic data using fit_lin() fit_nlin() functions. latter, alternative fit_nlin2() allows estimating third parameter, upper asymptote, maximum disease intensity close 100%. fit_multi() function flexible allows fit models multiple epidemic datasets. First, need load packages ’ll need tutorial.

previous_headings: Basic usage
title: Fitting and selecting models
text: use epifitter data least two variables needed, one representing time assessment disease intensity course epidemics representing disease intensity variable proportion (e.g. incidence, severity, prevalence). case designed experiments replicates, third variable needed. Let’s simulate DPC dataset one epidemics measured replicated plots. simulated data resemble polyciclic epidemics sigmoid shape. can using epifitter sim_logistic() function epifitter (?sim_logistic ). Let’s give look simulated dataset. dpc_L object generated using sim_logistic() dataframe four columns. y variable vector disease intensity proportion (0 < y < 1). facilitate visualization, let’s make plot using ggplot function ggplot2 package.
code: dpcL <- sim_logistic(   N = 100, # duration of the epidemics in days   y0 = 0.01, # disease intensity at time zero   dt = 10, # interval between assessments   r = 0.1, # apparent infection rate   alpha = 0.2, # level of noise   n = 7 # number of replicates ) head(dpcL) ##   replicates time          y   random_y ## 1          1    0 0.01000000 0.01000000 ## 2          1   10 0.02672668 0.02805496 ## 3          1   20 0.06946279 0.03795496 ## 4          1   30 0.16868385 0.16852760 ## 5          1   40 0.35549349 0.38397525 ## 6          1   50 0.59989486 0.65502345 ggplot(   dpcL,   aes(time, y,     group = replicates   ) ) +   geom_point(aes(time, random_y), shape = 1) + # plot the replicate values   geom_point(color = "steelblue", size = 2) +   geom_line(color = "steelblue") +   labs(     title = "Simulated 'complete' epidemics of sigmoid shape",     subtitle = "Produced using sim_logistic()"   )+   theme_minimal_hgrid()

previous_headings: Basic usage
title: Fitting and selecting models
text: fit_lin() requires least time y arguments. example, call random_y represents replicates. quick way call variables attached dataframe shown . fit_lin() outputs list object contains several elements. Three elements list shown default: stats model fit, Infection rate Initial Inoculum

previous_headings: Basic usage > Linear regression using fit_lin()
title: Fitting and selecting models
text: Stats element list shows four models predicted observations based three measures: Lin’s concordance correlation coefficient CCC (Lin 2000), measure agreement takes bias precision account Coefficient determination r_squared (R2), measure precision Residual standard deviation RSE model. four models sorted high low CCC. expected sim_logistic function used create synthetic epidemic data, Logistic model superior others.

previous_headings: Basic usage > Linear regression using fit_lin()
title: Fitting and selecting models
text: estimates, respective standard error upper lower 95% confidence interval, two coefficients interest shown Infection rate Initial inoculum elements. latter, back-transformed (estimate) linearized estimate shown.

previous_headings: Basic usage > Linear regression using fit_lin()
title: Fitting and selecting models
text: element f_lin$stats_all provides wide format dataframe stats model.
code: f_lin$stats_all ## # A tibble: 4 × 16 ##   best_CCC best_RSE model              r     r_se r_ci_lwr r_ci_upr    v0  v0_se ##      <dbl>    <dbl> <chr>          <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl> <dbl>  <dbl> ## 1        4        3 Exponential   0.0446 0.00225    0.0401   0.0491 -3.56 0.133  ## 2        2        2 Gompertz      0.0711 0.00176    0.0676   0.0746 -2.34 0.104  ## 3        1        1 Logistic      0.0996 0.000773   0.0981   0.101  -4.58 0.0457 ## 4        3        4 Monomolecular 0.0550 0.00234    0.0503   0.0596 -1.02 0.138  ## # ℹ 7 more variables: r_squared <dbl>, RSE <dbl>, CCC <dbl>, y0 <dbl>, ## #   y0_ci_lwr <dbl>, y0_ci_upr <dbl>, rho <dbl>

title: Fitting and selecting models
text: plot_fit() produces, default, panel plots depicting observed predicted values fitted models. arguments pont_size line_size control size dots observation size fitted line, respectively.
code: plot_lin <- plot_fit(f_lin,   point_size = 2,   line_size = 1 )   # Default plots plot_lin

previous_headings: Basic usage > Linear regression using fit_lin()
title: Fitting and selecting models
text: plots ggplot2 objects can easily customized adding new layers override plot paramaters shown . argument models allows select models(s) shown plot. next plot customized logistic model.
code: # Customized plots  plot_fit(f_lin,   point_size = 2,   line_size = 1,   models = "Logistic")+   theme_minimal_hgrid(font_size =18) +   scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0,100))+   scale_color_grey()+    theme(legend.position = "none")+   labs(     x = "Time",     y = "Proportion disease "        )

previous_headings: Basic usage > Non-linear regression
title: Fitting and selecting models
text: fit_nlin() function uses Levenberg-Marquardt algorithm least-squares estimation nonlinear parameters. addition time disease intensity, starting values y0 r given starting_par argument. output format interpretation analogous fit_lin(). NOTE: encounter error messages saying “matrix initial parameter estimates”, try modify starting values parameters solve problem. can check results using plot_fit.
code: f_nlin <- fit_nlin(   time = dpcL$time,   y = dpcL$random_y,   starting_par = list(y0 = 0.01, r = 0.03) )  f_nlin ## Results of fitting population models  ##  ## Stats: ##                  CCC r_squared    RSE ## Monomolecular 0.9104    0.8604 0.1583 ## Exponential   0.8869    0.8245 0.1758 ## Gompertz      0.9959    0.9933 0.0367 ## Logistic      0.9982    0.9963 0.0246 ##  ##  Infection rate: ##                 Estimate   Std.error      Lower      Upper         rho ## Monomolecular 0.02256068 0.001522488 0.01952773 0.01952773 0.011280341 ## Exponential   0.01916079 0.001407090 0.01635772 0.01635772 0.003193465 ## Gompertz      0.07226209 0.002129110 0.06802069 0.06802069 0.018065522 ## Logistic      0.10250959 0.002086494 0.09835308 0.09835308 0.017084931 ##  ##  Initial inoculum: ##                    Estimate    Std.error         Lower         Upper ## Monomolecular -1.921273e-01 4.726510e-02 -2.862843e-01 -9.797042e-02 ## Exponential    1.787016e-01 2.092668e-02  1.370135e-01  2.203897e-01 ## Gompertz       1.466893e-08 2.542713e-08 -3.598450e-08  6.532236e-08 ## Logistic       8.533242e-03 8.419049e-04  6.856081e-03  1.021040e-02 plot_fit(f_nlin) +   theme_minimal_hgrid()#changing plot theme

previous_headings: Basic usage
title: Fitting and selecting models
text: many epidemics last measure (final time) DPC reach maximum intensity , reason, estimation maximum asymptote (carrying capacity K) may necessary. fin_lin2() provides estimation K addition estimates provided fit_lin(). demonstrating function, can transform simulated data creating another variable y_random2 maximum 0.8 (80%). Simplest way multiply y_random 0.8. run fit_nlin2() new dataset.  NOTE: exponential model included doesn’t maximum asymptote. estimated value K expected 0.8.
code: dpcL2 = dpcL %>%    mutate(random_y = random_y * 0.8) f_nlin2 <- fit_nlin2(   time = dpcL2$time,   y = dpcL2$random_y,   starting_par = list(y0 = 0.01, r = 0.2, K =  0.6) ) f_nlin2 ## Results of fitting population models  ##  ## Stats: ##                  CCC r_squared    RSE ## Monomolecular 0.9433    0.8999 0.1047 ## Gompertz      0.9570    0.9600 0.0904 ## Logistic      0.9982    0.9963 0.0198 ##  ##  Infection rate: ##                 Estimate   Std.error       Lower       Upper         rho ## Monomolecular 0.01614355 0.003189458 0.009788412 0.009788412 0.008071774 ## Gompertz      0.10526637 0.016684801 0.072021174 0.072021174 0.017103039 ## Logistic      0.10267337 0.002532523 0.097627206 0.097627206 0.013680288 ##  ##  Initial inoculum: ##                    Estimate    Std.error         Lower         Upper ## Monomolecular -1.462051e-01 3.334841e-02 -2.126533e-01 -7.975695e-02 ## Gompertz       1.006691e-19 2.696818e-18 -5.272859e-18  5.474197e-18 ## Logistic       6.784477e-03 7.643834e-04  5.261410e-03  8.307544e-03 ##  ##  Maximum disease intensity: ##                Estimate   Std.error     Lower     Upper ## Monomolecular 1.0000000 0.112171775 0.7764929 1.2235071 ## Gompertz      0.6498957 0.018713653 0.6126079 0.6871834 ## Logistic      0.7994452 0.004854938 0.7897715 0.8091189 plot_fit(f_nlin2)

title: Fitting and selecting models
text: commonly, one epidemics analyse either observational experimental studies. goal fit common model curves, fit_multi() function hand. DPC needs unique identified combined single data frame.

previous_headings: Basic usage > Fit models to multiple DPCs
title: Fitting and selecting models
text: Let’s use sim_ family functions create three epidemics store data single data.frame. Gompertz model used simulate data. Note allowed y0 r parameter differ DPCs. combine three DPCs using bind_rows() function name identifier (.id), automatically created character vector, epidemics ‘DPC’. can visualize three DPCs plot  use facet_wrap() ploting separately.
code: epi1 <- sim_gompertz(N = 60, y0 = 0.001, dt = 5, r = 0.1, alpha = 0.4, n = 4) epi2 <- sim_gompertz(N = 60, y0 = 0.001, dt = 5, r = 0.12, alpha = 0.4, n = 4) epi3 <- sim_gompertz(N = 60, y0 = 0.003, dt = 5, r = 0.14, alpha = 0.4, n = 4)  multi_epidemic <- bind_rows(epi1,   epi2,   epi3,   .id = "DPC" ) head(multi_epidemic) ##   DPC replicates time          y  random_y ## 1   1          1    0 0.00100000 0.0010000 ## 2   1          1    5 0.01515505 0.0161830 ## 3   1          1   10 0.07878459 0.0761623 ## 4   1          1   15 0.21411521 0.3436388 ## 5   1          1   20 0.39266393 0.5165197 ## 6   1          1   25 0.56723412 0.6407593 p_multi <- ggplot(multi_epidemic,        aes(time, y, shape = DPC, group = DPC))+   geom_point(size =2)+   geom_line()+   theme_minimal_grid(font_size =18) +    labs(     x = "Time",     y = "Proportion disease "        ) p_multi p_multi +   facet_wrap(~ DPC, ncol = 1)

previous_headings: Basic usage > Fit models to multiple DPCs
title: Fitting and selecting models
text: fit_multi() requires least four arguments: time, disease intensity (proportion), data curve identifier (strata_cols). latter argument accepts one strata include c("strata1",strata2"). example , stratum name DPC, name variable. default, linear regression fitted data adding another argument nlin = T, non linear regressions fitted instead. parameters list can returned using $ operator . Similarly, data can returned. nonlinear regression preferred, nlim argument set TRUE

previous_headings: Basic usage > Fit models to multiple DPCs
title: Fitting and selecting models
text: want estimate K, set nlin = TRUE estimate_K = TRUE. NOTE: set arguments TRUE, K estimated, nlin defaut FALSE. Also remember estimating K, don’t fit Exponential model.

previous_headings: Basic usage > Graphical outputs
title: Fitting and selecting models
text: original data predicted values model stored multi_fit$Data. nice plot can produced follows:  Using dplyr function filter model interest can chosen plotting.
code: multi_fit$Data %>%   ggplot(aes(time, predicted, color = DPC)) +   geom_point(aes(time, y), color = "gray") +   geom_line(size = 1) +   facet_grid(DPC ~ model, scales = "free_y") +   theme_minimal_hgrid()+   coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0, 1)) ## Warning: Using `size` aesthetic for lines was deprecated in ggplot2 3.4.0. ## ℹ Please use `linewidth` instead. ## This warning is displayed once per session. ## Call `lifecycle::last_lifecycle_warnings()` to see where this warning was ## generated. multi_fit$Data %>%   filter(model == "Gompertz") %>%   ggplot(aes(time, predicted, color = DPC)) +   geom_point(aes(time, y),     color = "gray",     size = 2   ) +   geom_line(size = 1.2) +   theme_minimal_hgrid() +   labs(     x = "Time",     y = "Disease Intensity"   )

previous_headings: Basic usage > Graphical outputs
title: Fitting and selecting models
text: multi_fit$Parameters element stats parameters stored. Let’s plot estimates apparent infection rate.
code: multi_fit$Parameters %>%   filter(model == "Gompertz") %>%   ggplot(aes(DPC, r)) +   geom_point(size = 3) +   geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = r_ci_lwr, ymax = r_ci_upr),     width = 0,     size = 1   ) +   labs(     x = "Time",     y = "Apparent infection rate"   ) +   theme_minimal_hgrid()

title: Simulating disease progress curves
text: epiffiter, opposite fit_ functions (estimate parameters fitting models data), sim_ family functions allows produce DPC data given set parameters specific model. Currently, four population dynamic models fitted data can simulated. functions use ode() function devolve package (Soetaert,Petzoldt & Setzer 2010) solve differential equation form e epidemiological models.

previous_headings: Hands On
title: Simulating disease progress curves
text: First, need load packages ’ll need tutorial.

previous_headings: Hands On
title: Simulating disease progress curves
text: sim_ functions, regardless model, require set six arguments. default, least two arguments required (others default values) r: apparent infection rate n: number replicates n greater one, replicated epidemics (e.g. replicated treatments) produced level noise (experimental error) set alpha argument. two arguments combined set generate random_y values, vary randomly across defined number replicates. arguments : N: epidemic duration time units dt: time (fixed) units two assessments y0: initial inoculum alpha: noise parameters replicates

previous_headings: Hands On > The basics of the simulation
title: Simulating disease progress curves
text: Let’s simulate curve resembling exponential growth. data.frame object produced four columns: replicates: curve respective ID number time: assessment time y: simulated proportion disease intensity random_y: randomly simulated proportion disease intensity based noise Use ggplot2 package build impressive graphics!
code: exp_model <- sim_exponential(   N = 100,    # total time units    y0 = 0.01,  # initial inoculum   dt = 10,    #  interval between assessments in time units   r = 0.045,  #  apparent infection rate   alpha = 0.2,# level of noise   n = 7       # number of replicates ) head(exp_model) ##   replicates time          y   random_y ## 1          1    0 0.01000000 0.01000000 ## 2          1   10 0.01568425 0.01648514 ## 3          1   20 0.02459905 0.01260818 ## 4          1   30 0.03858028 0.03853729 ## 5          1   40 0.06050749 0.06802921 ## 6          1   50 0.09489670 0.11669280 exp_plot = exp_model %>%   ggplot(aes(time, y)) +   geom_jitter(aes(time, random_y), size = 3,color = "gray", width = .1) +   geom_line(size = 1) +   theme_minimal_hgrid() +   ylim(0,1)+   labs(     title = "Exponential",     y = "Disease intensity",     x = "Time"   ) ## Warning: Using `size` aesthetic for lines was deprecated in ggplot2 3.4.0. ## ℹ Please use `linewidth` instead. ## This warning is displayed once per session. ## Call `lifecycle::last_lifecycle_warnings()` to see where this warning was ## generated. exp_plot ## Warning: Removed 1 row containing missing values or values outside the scale range ## (`geom_point()`).

previous_headings: Hands On > The basics of the simulation
title: Simulating disease progress curves
text: logic exactly .
code: mono_model <- sim_monomolecular(   N = 100,   y0 = 0.01,   dt = 5,   r = 0.05,   alpha = 0.2,   n = 7 ) head(mono_model) ##   replicates time         y   random_y ## 1          1    0 0.0100000 0.01034092 ## 2          1    5 0.2289861 0.22579664 ## 3          1   10 0.3995322 0.49186456 ## 4          1   15 0.5323535 0.59700132 ## 5          1   20 0.6357949 0.67047285 ## 6          1   25 0.7163551 0.73895902 mono_plot = mono_model %>%   ggplot(aes(time, y)) +   geom_jitter(aes(time, random_y), size = 3, color = "gray", width = .1) +   geom_line(size = 1) +   theme_minimal_hgrid() +   labs(     title = "Monomolecular",     y = "Disease intensity",     x = "Time"   ) mono_plot

code: logist_model <- sim_logistic(   N = 100,   y0 = 0.01,   dt = 5,   r = 0.1,   alpha = 0.2,   n = 7 ) head(logist_model) ##   replicates time          y   random_y ## 1          1    0 0.01000000 0.01067404 ## 2          1    5 0.01638216 0.01485875 ## 3          1   10 0.02672677 0.03041405 ## 4          1   15 0.04331509 0.03064342 ## 5          1   20 0.06946352 0.07253287 ## 6          1   25 0.10958806 0.08396757 logist_plot = logist_model %>%   ggplot(aes(time, y)) +   geom_jitter(aes(time, random_y), size = 3,color = "gray", width = .1) +   geom_line(size = 1) +   theme_minimal_hgrid() +   labs(     title = "Logistic",     y = "Disease intensity",     x = "Time"   ) logist_plot

code: gomp_model <- sim_gompertz(   N = 100,   y0 = 0.01,   dt = 5,   r = 0.07,   alpha = 0.2,   n = 7 ) head(gomp_model) ##   replicates time          y  random_y ## 1          1    0 0.01000000 0.0100000 ## 2          1    5 0.03896283 0.0475927 ## 3          1   10 0.10158896 0.1089836 ## 4          1   15 0.19958740 0.1845412 ## 5          1   20 0.32122825 0.3154174 ## 6          1   25 0.44922018 0.5099217 gomp_plot = gomp_model %>%   ggplot(aes(time, y)) +   geom_jitter(aes(time, random_y), size = 3,color = "gray", width = .1) +   geom_line(size = 1) +   theme_minimal_hgrid() +   labs(     title = "Gompertz",     y = "Disease intensity",     x = "Time"   ) gomp_plot

what: An R package for analysis and simulation of plant disease progress curves
title: An R package for analysis and simulation of plant disease progress curves
text: Provides set tools aiding visualization, description, comparison plant disease progress curve (DPC) data. DPC depict change disease-intensity variable measured sequentially different times epidemics. analysis, may include fitting “classic” population dynamics models (e.g. logistic, monomolecular, Gompertz), allows gaining understanding epidemiological processes, used comparing epidemics. mathematics behind model fitting straightforward general-purpose spreadsheet statistical softwares can used perform calculations select “best” model. epifitter provide analyst R functions performing several tasks commonly used temporal analysis epidemics, including graphical output. Current implementation includes functions analyst : Fit classic population dynamics models using linear nonlinear approaches Select models based statistical visual analysis Calculate area disease progress curve Compare epidemics via visual inference Simulate synthetic epidemics various shapes uncertainty

title: An R package for analysis and simulation of plant disease progress curves
text: Install stable release CRAN. development version epifitter available GitHub. devtools package, available CRAN, required installation.

title: An R package for analysis and simulation of plant disease progress curves
text: Please report issues bugs. code licensed MIT cite epifitter, please use output citation(“epifitter”) Please note epifitter released Contributor Code Conduct. participating project agree abide terms.

title: Estimate disease progress curve from only two points — AUDPC2_points
Estimate disease progress curve from only two points — AUDPC2_points
text: time Number days first disease assessment last disease assessment. y0 numeric value representing disease intensity first intensity measurement. Must greater zero less one. yT numeric value representing disease intensity last intensity measurement. Must greater y0 less one.

title: Estimate disease progress curve from only two points — AUDPC2_points
text: Jeger, M. J., & Viljanen-Rollinson, S. L. H. (2001). use area disease-progress curve (AUDPC) assess quantitative disease resistance crop cultivars. *Theoretical Applied Genetics*, 102, 32-40. DOI: 10.1007/s001220051615.

text: Adam H. Sparks, adamhsparks@gmail.com

title: Estimate disease progress curve from only two points — AUDPC2_points
code: epi <- sim_logistic(N = 30, y0 = 0.01, dt = 5, r = 0.3, alpha = 0.5, n = 1)  # compare with use values from `epi`, first and last only # time epi$time[7] #> [1] 30  # y0 epi$y[1] #> [1] 0.01  # yT epi$y[7] #> [1] 0.9879302  AUDPC_2_points(time = epi$time[7], y0 = epi$y[1], yT = epi$y[7]) #> [1] 14.68996  # compare with traitional AUDPC trapezoidal method AUDPC(time = epi$time, y = epi$y, y_proportion = TRUE) #> [1] 14.69147

code: fit_multi(time_col,              intensity_col,              data,              strata_cols ,              starting_par = list(y0 = 0.01, r = 0.03, K =  0.8),              maxiter=500,              nlin = FALSE,              estimate_K = FALSE)

title: Estimate model parameters for multiple disease progress curves — fit_multi
text: time_col Character name specifying column time. eg: time_col = "days". intensity_col Character name specifying column disease intensity. data data.frame object containing variables model fitting. strata_cols Character name vector specifying columns stratification. starting_par Starting value initial inoculun (y0) apparent infection rate (r). Please informe especific order maxiter Maximum number iterations. used nlin = TRUE nlin Logical. FALSE estimates parameters using data linearization. nlin=TRUE, estimates nonlinear approach. fit_nlin. estimate_K Logical. nlin=TRUE, estimates maximum disease intensity. (default: nlin=FALSE) fit_nlin2.

title: Estimate model parameters for multiple disease progress curves — fit_multi
text: Returns data.frame containing estimated parameters individual strata levels.

title: Fits epidemic models using nonlinear aproach — fit_nlin

title: Fits epidemic models using nonlinear aproach — fit_nlin
text: Kaique dos S. Alves

title: Fits epidemic models using nonlinear aproach — fit_nlin
code: set.seed(1) epi1 <- sim_logistic(N = 30,                      y0 = 0.01,                      dt = 5,                      r = 0.3,                      alpha = 0.5,                      n = 4) data = data.frame(time =  epi1[,2], y = epi1[,4]) fit_nlin(time = data$time, y =  data$y, starting_par = list(y0 = 0.001, r = 0.03), maxiter = 1024) #> Warning: NaNs produced #> Warning: NaNs produced #> Warning: NaNs produced #> Warning: NaNs produced #> Warning: NaNs produced #> Warning: NaNs produced #> Warning: NaNs produced #> Warning: NaNs produced #> Warning: NaNs produced #> Warning: NaNs produced #> Warning: NaNs produced #> Warning: NaNs produced #> Results of fitting population models  #>  #> Stats: #>                  CCC r_squared    RSE #> Monomolecular 0.8960    0.8361 0.1762 #> Exponential   0.8795    0.8131 0.1880 #> Gompertz      0.9950    0.9909 0.0429 #> Logistic      0.9923    0.9860 0.0529 #>  #>  Infection rate: #>                 Estimate   Std.error      Lower      Upper        rho #> Monomolecular 0.06822917 0.008441786 0.05087684 0.05087684 0.03411459 #> Exponential   0.06438233 0.008534818 0.04683876 0.04683876 0.01073039 #> Gompertz      0.26304504 0.015218902 0.23176214 0.23176214 0.06576126 #> Logistic      0.36613264 0.028677439 0.30718532 0.30718532 0.06102211 #>  #>  Initial inoculum: #>                    Estimate    Std.error         Lower        Upper #> Monomolecular -1.487705e-01 7.778021e-02 -3.086500e-01 1.110901e-02 #> Exponential    1.658502e-01 3.643013e-02  9.096701e-02 2.407334e-01 #> Gompertz       1.017193e-12 5.947383e-12 -1.120783e-11 1.324221e-11 #> Logistic       5.304364e-03 2.219734e-03  7.416344e-04 9.867093e-03

title: Fits epidemic models using nonlinear aproach. This function also estimates the maximum disease intensity parameter K — fit_nlin2
text: time Numeric vector refers time steps epidemics. y Numeric vector refers disease intensity. starting_par starting value initial inoculun (y0) apparent infection rate (r), maximum disease intensity (K). Please informe especific order maxiter Maximun number iterations.

title: Fits epidemic models using nonlinear aproach. This function also estimates the maximum disease intensity parameter K — fit_nlin2
code: set.seed(1)  epi1 <- sim_logistic(N = 30,                      y0 = 0.01,                      dt = 5,                      r = 0.3,                      alpha = 0.5,                      n = 4)  data = data.frame(time =  epi1[,2], y = epi1[,4]) fit_nlin2(time = data$time,           y =  data$y,           starting_par = list(y0 = 0.01, r = 0.03, K = 1),           maxiter = 1024) #> Warning: NaNs produced #> Warning: NaNs produced #> Warning: NaNs produced #> Warning: NaNs produced #> Warning: NaNs produced #> Warning: NaNs produced #> Warning: NaNs produced #> Warning: NaNs produced #> Warning: NaNs produced #> Warning: NaNs produced #> Warning: NaNs produced #> Warning: NaNs produced #> Results of fitting population models  #>  #> Stats: #>                  CCC r_squared    RSE #> Monomolecular 0.8960    0.8361 0.1797 #> Gompertz      0.9950    0.9909 0.0438 #> Logistic      0.9923    0.9860 0.0539 #>  #>  Infection rate: #>                Estimate  Std.error      Lower      Upper        rho #> Monomolecular 0.0682291 0.02690929 0.01280837 0.01280837 0.03411455 #> Gompertz      0.2630455 0.02071581 0.22038054 0.22038054 0.06576138 #> Logistic      0.3661332 0.03408759 0.29592845 0.29592845 0.06102219 #>  #>  Initial inoculum: #>                    Estimate    Std.error         Lower        Upper #> Monomolecular -1.487701e-01 8.495704e-02 -3.237424e-01 2.620220e-02 #> Gompertz       1.017021e-12 7.351869e-12 -1.412444e-11 1.615848e-11 #> Logistic       5.304329e-03 2.520782e-03  1.126822e-04 1.049598e-02 #>  #>  Maximum disease intensity: #>               Estimate  Std.error     Lower    Upper #> Monomolecular        1 0.19490764 0.5985802 1.401420 #> Gompertz             1 0.01936234 0.9601225 1.039877 #> Logistic             1 0.02080931 0.9571424 1.042858

title: Creates a plot panel for the fitted models — plot_fit
Creates a plot panel for the fitted models — plot_fit
text: object fit_lin fit_nlin object point_size Point size line_size Line size models Select models displayed panel

title: Creates a plot panel for the fitted models — plot_fit
text: possible add ggplot components using + syntax. See examples .

title: Creates a plot panel for the fitted models — plot_fit
code: epi1 <- sim_logistic(N = 30,                      y0 = 0.01,                      dt = 5,                      r = 0.3,                      alpha = 0.5,                      n = 4) data = data.frame(time =  epi1[,2], y = epi1[,4]) fitted = fit_lin( time = data$time, y =  data$y) plot_fit(fitted)   # adding ggplot components library(ggplot2) plot_fit(fitted)+   theme_minimal()+   ylim(0,1)+   labs(y = "Disease internsity", x = "Time") #> Warning: Removed 18 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range #> (`geom_line()`). #> Warning: Removed 18 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range #> (`geom_line()`).
